metadata.title: Topic Modeling: Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences

abstract: Objective. Topic modeling (TM) refers to a group of methods for mathematically identifying latent topics in large corpora of data. Although TM shows promise as a tool for social science research, most researchers lack awareness of the tool's utility. Therefore, this article provides a brief overview of TM's logic and processes, offers a simple example, and suggests several possible uses in social sciences. Methods. Using latent semantic analysis in our example, we analyzed transcripts of the 2016 U.S. presidential debates between Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump. Results. Resulting topics paralleled the most frequent policy-related Internet searches at the time. When divided by candidate, changes in emergent topics reflected individual policy stances, with nuanced differences between the two. Conclusion. Findings underscored the utility of TM to identify thematic patterns embedded in large quantities of text. TM, therefore, represents a valuable addition to the social scientist's methodological tool set.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: Estimates suggest scientific journals publish a new article every 20 seconds (Munroe, Kaiser, and Malakoff, 2013). Given such volume of research information, researchers need new strategies to compensate for humans' limited processing abilities. How can one wade through, dissect, and interpret extraordinarily large amounts of information while avoiding what some have termed "infoxication" or "infobesity" (Benito-Ruiz, 2009;Brophy and Bawden, 2005)? 1 Topic modeling (TM), one useful set of methods developed by computer scientists, provides a systematic tool for synthesizing large quantities of text/language data into manageable and interpretable segments (Blei, Ng, and Jordan, 2003). Herein we argue for the use of TM in the social sciences, specifically as a strategy for managing and exploring otherwise exorbitant quantities of textual data.

The label "topic modeling" applies to various methods designed to uncover latent themes and thematic structures within a large collection of text-based documents, or a corpus, using mathematical algorithms. Despite technical differences, all methods share this common purpose. At its core, TM grounds itself in the notion that both word choice and placement are intentional. Thus, mathematically modeling words in relation to the words with which they occur in a given text allows the unveiling of underlying, not immediately observable concepts, assumptions, or intentions.

Although scientists are not able to read/process all available information in their fields, nor easily detect latent patterns within that information, computers can. Computer algorithms used in TM identify themes related to (otherwise hidden) word patterns occurring across large amounts of data. When the data comprise words and text, then, the topic model maps words by similarity and co-occurrence, with particular attention to proximity among/between words in that corpus.

For example, imagine a statistician-in-training wishing to assess the emphasis being given to null-hypothesis significance testing. It is unlikely this apprentice could read and process all information available. Even if he/she restricted the literature to, say, statistics journals with high impact factors published since 1990, the task would remain insurmountable. TM, however, makes the attempt less daunting. Using TM, the student may uncover salient themes, such as "sample size," "statistical power," "effect sizes," and "Sir Ronald Fisher," reflecting the relative importance of these themes in the field.

TM's primary benefit, when compared against traditional content analytic methods, is its ability to decrease researcher subjectivity and enhance analytic efficiency. A central limitation of traditional content analysis (and systematic forms of literature reviews) is the difficulty in removing human error or bias (Downe-Wamboldt, 1992;Neuendorf, 2016). Human coding of texts/content is shaped by the computational limits of the researcher's mind, time constraints, the researcher's understanding, and his/her ability to detect latent biases. TM's utility, therefore, lies in computers' precision in understanding, consolidating, and learning the semantics of text-based data too large for a single researcher or a team.

These characteristics-precision, objectivity, and scope-render TM a powerful tool for social scientists. To date, however, TM's use in applied and social sciences remains scant. Among the published literature, we see TM employed rather exclusively in marketing (Zhang, Kim, and Xing, 2015), digital communication, or social media studies (Gao, Li, and Darwish, 2012;Hong and Davison, 2010;Zhao et al., 2011), and within its original field: computer science (Blei, Ng, and Jordan, 2003;Blei, 2015;Blei and Lafferty, 2006;Brophy and Bawden, 2005;Wallach, 2006). While some authors have suggested its use in subdisciplines (see Grimmer and Stewart, 2013), to the best of our knowledge, TM has not been widely adopted in the social sciences.

We argue, therefore, that social scientists should learn about TM and apply its methods in their research. We aim, in this article, to raise awareness of this novel method, its characteristics and applications, with the intent of promoting its use by social scientists. To achieve this aim, we first introduce the logic of TM, with primary emphasis on one particular framework we believe could be readily applied across disciplines: latent semantic analysis (LSA). Next, we provide an example illustrating LSA's capabilities. We discuss the benefits of applying TM to social sciences research, and recommend specific applications, also.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: When text documents compose a corpus, TM maps the co-occurrence of words to map that corpus' underlying semantic structure. Though various algorithms for producing topic models ultimately perform the same task, they arrive at solutions through different mechanisms. While a discussion of every TM method is beyond our scope, we will discuss the underlying logic in one of the more common methods: LSA. We chose to explore this method in particular because it is a preferred choice for many scientists and does not employ proprietary algorithms, unlike other methods, which are not freely available for public use (e.g., Leximancer).

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: While various TM methods are computationally different, their purpose is similar: to reduce voluminous data by synthesizing the most salient information into hierarchically organized themes or topics. TM also addresses specific weaknesses in traditional content analyses, such as word counts. One particular difficulty associated with word counting is polysemy, that is, multiple meanings for a single word. For example, consider instances of the word "regression" in a corpus of studies on school standardized testing scores. Here, "regression" may refer to backward movement of scores, as when students perform worse on a test (i.e., there was "score regression" among students). However, "regression" may also refer to the statistical method employed to predict outcomes among data. In this case, a simple word count would not differentiate between competing meanings of the term "regression," both of which appear in the corpus. Because it examines the co-occurrence of terms and their proximity to each other, TM maps both meanings and allows the researcher to note both occurrences. As frequency of occurrence, alone, is not sufficient for differentiating meaning, the inclusion of lexical context allows for a more nuanced analysis of a term-and TM both accounts for and captures these contexts.

Another of TM's important features is how it evaluates terms at multiple levels of analysis to determine the themes or topics in a corpus. First, each word in a corpus is assigned a weight, based on estimates of the point coordinate (i.e., how close or far a word is from other words), word frequency (how often the word is used), and how the word is contextualized (the semantics of the word). Topic models then examine: (a) which words have the largest weight, (b) which words lie in closest proximity to words with the highest weights, and (c) how salient, or apparent, is a given cluster of words due to frequency. Depending on how heavily weighted (i.e., important) a given cluster of words is, the cluster can become one of k latent topics in the corpus.

A topic, then, consists of a collection of words that the TM identifies as (a) important, (b) occurring in close proximity to one another, and (c) frequently co-occurring as a unit throughout the corpus. The topics become one of the elements reported in the TM computation's output.

Another important process common across most modeling techniques is stemming. In this process, words are shortened and reduced to their stem, which conveys the meaning of the word. For example, if we ran a topic model of the current article, stemming of the word "modeling" would be important. Done during preparation for analysis, stemming reduces words such as "models," "modeled," and "modeling" to "model." This shortened form minimizes redundancy in the topics, such that only the root word will appear in the final topic model. Also removed from the corpus are stop words (e.g., certain prepositions, conjunctions) that, although important, offer nothing in terms of content and can, therefore, be ignored.

Finally, as with any statistical method, topic models require researchers to engage in thoughtful, a priori decisions regarding (a) which document inclusion criteria to apply, (b) how many topics should the TP extract, and (c) what theoretical framework to invoke for interpretation. Similar to other content analytic methods, TM provides results grounded entirely in the input provided. Therefore, a well-defined inclusion criteria and thorough search process are critical. Further, in TM approaches, the number of latent topics generated (k) is predetermined by the researcher. Deciding how many topics are relevant for a data set represents a delicate balance between specificity and interpretability-with the best solution being the one most useful to the specific project. Similar to exploratory factor analysis (EFA), TM and the topics extracted from the corpus are purposefully constructed. Therefore, interpreting the topics is just as important as identifying them (Wang and Grimson, 2008).

Finally, researchers must attribute meaning to the resulting themes, through naming the topics generated. As with EFA, authors may rely on interrater agreement or other validity checks for naming the topics (Cochrane, n.d.).

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: LSA, a particular TM method, adopts a multistep process for approximating a corpus' structure by identifying and mathematically modeling the context(s) in which a term appears (Sidorova et al., 2008;Evangelopoulos, Zhang, and Prybutok, 2012). Conceptually, LSA is similar to traditional EFA in reducing the corpus to a series of eigenvectors that can be compared and employs the following steps. First, a document-term matrix (DTM) comprising word counts per paragraph is developed. LSA then employs a mathematical method known as singular value decomposition (SVD) to reduce this matrix. Through SVD, eigenvectors for each word are approximated and then evaluated to determine how close/distal are the words in their multidimensional vector space (Dumais, 2004;Landauer, 2006). In other words, by comparing the angular association in the vector space between two words' eigenvectors, one can approximate their relative similarity. As with EFA, LSA uses a factor rotation of the resulting eigenvectors to create a nonoverlapping fit for the model (Visinescu and Evangelopoulos, 2014). Finally, topics are derived from the relative clustering of resulting word eigenvectors throughout the corpus.

An important advantage for many social scientists who may not have access to supercomputers is the fact LSA requires relatively less computing power than other TM methods (as most of its estimations utilize eigenvector matrices, instead of estimating probabilities of occurrence for each word individually). However, this advantage is counterbalanced by LSA's inability to estimate a structure from the raw data, directly. Given that (a) the only observed data in the topic model are the words themselves and (b) LSA first derives eigenvectors, then analyzes spatial differences in the factor space, LSA's level of analysis is further removed from the actual data when compared to other methods. Therefore, as with any statistical procedure, researchers must carefully consider each method's relative benefits and tradeoffs.

First, a document-term matrix (DTM) comprising word counts per paragraph is developed. LSA then employs a mathematical method known as singular value decomposition (SVD) to reduce this matrix. Through SVD, eigenvectors for each word are approximated and then evaluated to determine how close/distal are the words in their multidimensional vector space (Dumais, 2004;Landauer, 2006). In other words, by comparing the angular association in the vector space between two words' eigenvectors, one can approximate their relative similarity. As with EFA, LSA uses a factor rotation of the resulting eigenvectors to create a nonoverlapping fit for the model (Visinescu and Evangelopoulos, 2014). Finally, topics are derived from the relative clustering of resulting word eigenvectors throughout the corpus.

An important advantage for many social scientists who may not have access to supercomputers is the fact LSA requires relatively less computing power than other TM methods (as most of its estimations utilize eigenvector matrices, instead of estimating probabilities of occurrence for each word individually). However, this advantage is counterbalanced by LSA's inability to estimate a structure from the raw data, directly. Given that (a) the only observed data in the topic model are the words themselves and (b) LSA first derives eigenvectors, then analyzes spatial differences in the factor space, LSA's level of analysis is further removed from the actual data when compared to other methods. Therefore, as with any statistical procedure, researchers must carefully consider each method's relative benefits and tradeoffs.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
sections.header_raw: Heuristic Example Using the 2016 U.S. Presidential Debates
sections.header_norm: heuristic example using the 2016 u s presidential debates
paragraphs: The 2016 U.S. presidential election occurred during a time of high contention and strongly polarized views. In fact, since 1994, polarization between both political parties increased to the point where 92 percent of Republicans are to the right of the centrist Democrat, and 94 percent of Democrats fall to the left of centrist Republicans (Pew Research Center, 2015). This large distance between the two main parties and their respective candidates' views on common issues allow for a clear demonstration of LSA.

It is important to note that in this example we do not provide in-depth analysis, commentary, or value judgments about candidates and their positions, as an extensive examination of election rhetoric falls beyond our scope. The marked political differences between Democrats and Republicans and the style in which then candidates Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump communicated their platforms, however, are optimal instances to demonstrate how TM functions.

There were three debates between candidates Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton sponsored by the Commission on Presidential Debates. Each debate varied in focus and format, but all were transcribed and archived online by Politico (see web links in the reference section). These transcripts served as the corpus for the current study.

We sought to answer two specific research questions about the corpus and the predictive power of TM: (RQ1) "Would the emergent topics align with policy-related Internet searches ahead of the presidential election," and (RQ2) "How differently do the politicians react to the same question?"

To demonstrate the LSA procedure and its semantic capability, we first mapped the thematic structure of the entire corpus (both candidates' statements). Then, we sought to compare the most salient topics discussed by each candidate, individually. Therefore, we generated three topic models: (a) one model of all three debates with both Trump and Clinton included, (b) a t model of all three debates using only words spoken by Clinton, and (c) a model of all three debates using only words spoken by Donald Trump.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: We collected transcripts from the debates from Politico. To ensure accuracy, we then compared those transcripts to those from several other online news outlets. After this accuracy check, we cleaned the data by removing moderator questions and transcription notes, leaving only the candidates' words in the corpus.

In a second round of cleaning, we removed common stop words (n = 110 words). On the third round, we stemmed and parsed words and, finally, we ran the three models. In our first set of analyses, the observational unit was broadest: we explored salient topics spoken across the entirety of the debates. However, in our second analysis, we increased the granularity of the analyses, exploring the words spoken by each candidate.

The remaining terms are indexed into what is called a DTM. A DTM will include every word intended for analysis and the frequency with which the word was used. For example, in the debate corpus, a word like "country" would have a high frequency (the word was spoken more than 200 times). Conversely, rarely used words would have a far lower count, representing their reduced importance in the corpus.

Based on word frequencies and eigenvector proximities, the DTM is usually converted into an analogous mathematical adjacency matrix, with zeros across the diagonal, to determine where word clusters fall in a multidimensional space. The algorithm then uses the information afforded through the adjacency matrix and input from the user (e.g., number of topics and words per topic) to generate the resulting topics from the original corpus of data, subject to user-specified parameters.

Similar to traditional factor analysis, it is the responsibility of the researcher to assign meaning to these collections of words. Without semantic interpretation of what the word clusters mean or symbolize, the output is simply a collection of words. During the interpretation phase, there is always potential for biases to influence the meaning of the topics. When conducting a topic model, one should rely on validity checks, either from content experts or other supporting data, to bolster interpretation.

Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: To model the words spoken by both candidates across all three debates, our analysis reduced the large collection of words (n = 41,121) to 25 most salient topics (k = 25).

Table 1 lists the topics in order of importance based on their saliency within the corpus 15406237, 2018, 5, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/ssqu.12528 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [09/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

TABLE 1 All Debates, Both Participants Topic Number Theme Text Topic Terms per Topic Weight Scope 1 Emails campaign, +email, +talk, +state, +good and divided further by topic name, most salient words per topic, number of actual words associated with that topic, eigenvector or weight of each topic, and scope of each topic (i.e., domestic and international topics). Each category provides information regarding a topic's relative importance and, more importantly, shows how the words in the topics operate semantically to substantiate the content of the topics.

Because TM is able to identify important themes in a corpus, we would expect the central topics in this example to mirror salient political issues of the time. We compared, therefore, the topics produced by LSA to the questions and answers posed online by the public during the time of the presidential debates (RQ1). Prior to the election, U.S. News and World Report conducted an assessment of the most commonly searched/Googled policy-related terms and found these terms included, from June 23 to 29, 2016: (a) immigration, (b) same-sex marriage, (c) education, (d) gun control, (e) taxes, (f ) the economy, (g) healthcare, (h) climate change, (i) foreign policy, and (j) the environment (Cook, 2017).

In our analysis, most topics identified in the corpus related to one or more of those policy-related terms. For instance, one top policy issue according to U.S. News and World Report-foreign policy-relates to our model's emergent Topic 4: diplomatic issues (Iran, Russia, Assad, billion, +rebel).foot_1 This topic similarity provides two types of support for the model's validity. First, the similarity suggests TM can accurately learn and identify topics that are latent within its source material.

Second, TM exhibits impressive specificity in identifying topics. In particular, two instances of polysemy are parsed out in this example; these instances would not have been distinguishable using traditional word counts. First, gun violence appears twice in the model, emerging most clearly in Topic 12-gun violence (amendment, second, gun, +justice, +appoint). In this instance, gun control is discussed in the contexts of the Second Amendment and the possible effects of appointing a new Supreme Court justice to the then-vacant seat. However, gun violence also appears in Topic 7-Chicago violence (gun, Chicago, police, +kill, +bring). In this topic, the context for addressing gun violence was Chicago's inner-city crime rates, which became a common talking point in the nationwide debates on gun violence, police shootings, and legislative reform.

Another example is taxation, which, in the topic model, appears in Topic 5 (economic policy) and Topic 9 (tax return). Here, too, TM was able to identify taxation in two different domains. The more salient topic, economic policy (tax, middle, class, economy, middle, class), refers to discussions regarding the future of the U.S. middle class. However, the second topic, tax returns (release, +tax, +return, audit), emerges from a different context: Clinton's challenging of Trump to release undisclosed tax returns. Because Clinton broached the topic of Trump's tax returns on multiple occasions in each debate, this became a salient narrative in the election.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
sections.header_raw: Rival Topic Models: Clinton Versus Trump
sections.header_norm: rival topic models clinton versus trump
paragraphs: The topic model presented above demonstrated consistency between the topics identified and the important policy questions most frequently searched online during the year prior to the election. While these general themes are important, more specific questions can be addressed by adjusting the granularity of analysis. That is, as the level of analysis within a corpus is made smaller, different themes emerge, offering insights into the effects that particular level of analysis has on the overall model. To illustrate this in our example, we took the debate corpus and divided it in two: one portion containing only Clinton's words; the other, only Trump's. In doing so, we sought to explore how, within the presidential debates, the two candidates spoke differently about issues and how such differences might be captured in emergent topics (RQ2). The resulting topic models for each can be seen in Tables 2 (Clinton) and 3 (Trump).

Before the data were cleaned and stemmed, we determined that Clinton spoke 19,171 words (1,650 unique words) and Trump, 23,950 (1,147 unique words). Overall, the resulting topic models suggest Clinton spoke primarily about domestic policy, while Trump spoke about domestic and international affairs, in nearly equal amounts.

One of the most salient topics in the general (combined tests) model is related to gun control, a topic that was similarly salient in both candidates' individual topic models. For Clinton, the concept emerged in Topic 6-gun control-and, for Trump, in Topic 1-Supreme Court appointment. Both candidates referred often to the Second Amendment, particularly as it relates to the right to gun ownership. Clinton discussed the need for stronger regulation of guns, particularly with regard to closing certain regulatory loopholes in mandatory background checks, and the words highlighted in the topic clearly reflect this: gun, second, amendment, +loophole, close. However, Trump often suggested his opponent sought to weaken the Second Amendment and, therefore, appointing a new Supreme Court justice who would protect the constitutional right to bear arms would be important. This slant in Trump's speech also was captured by his Topic 1: amendment, +justice, second, +appoint, important.

Although both candidates' negative speech about each other emerged as salient topics in the combined model, slightly different patterns appear in the individual models. Clinton's argument that Trump should release his tax returns emerged in the combined model as Topic 9-tax return (release, +tax, +return, +tax return, audit). In Clinton's model, however, the word "audit" does not appear. It is also interesting to note that tax returns did not emerge as a salient topic in Trump's model, indicating that he largely avoided speaking about the topic; in Clinton's model, the matter of tax returns emerges as Topic 4 (Table 2).

A similar trend emerged with regard to Trump's detraction of Clinton, primarily centered on the use of a private email server during her tenure as Secretary of State. This theme emerged in the general model as Topic 20-email (wrong, +email, +delete, +fact, +know). In Trump's model, this theme is represented in Topic 10 (email, subpoena, +state, congress, united). In the latter, the language captured in the topic is stronger than that captured by the combined model, as Trump overtly called for a subpoena of Clinton's emails and referenced congressional investigations taking place at the time. Conversely, in Clinton's individual model, the term "email" never emerges as salient, suggesting a similar tactic to Trump's, of avoiding discussing potentially unethical matters.

This analysis using separate models for the partitioned data, therefore, demonstrates subtle differences that may not be depicted in a larger combined model. Such refinement of the information lends support for the exploration of topics at different levels of analysis (e.g., splitting a corpus into subgroups, time periods, or contrasting characteristics). In the example above, if so desired, we could further compare the data, to learn how the candidates' responses changed from debate to debate. It could also be informative to compare the models for candidates' stump speeches to these models we generated for the debates, in order to contrast discourses elicited within a fixed, prescribed format with those articulated within more flexible contexts. date, the use of TM has been limited, and mostly exploratory in nature. However, given its ability to unearth the underlying thematic structure of large amounts of data, we contend TM is a powerful tool, applicable to a number of research contexts, especially the social sciences. As a method, TM can quantify data that are otherwise difficult to objectively measure (e.g., language). For example, in the context of LSA, by measuring the angular distance between eigenvectors, one could quantify the relationship between topics, allowing for a deeper semantic understanding. Thus, TM's potential utility falls well beyond its present exploratory scope, across the social sciences.

While not exhaustive, here we propose three social sciences domains in which researchers could employ and expand the use of TM: (a) as a tool for reducing unintentional reviewer bias in systematic literature reviewing, (b) for practical thematic exploration of qualitative data and thematic analysis validation, and (c) for comparing similar corpora to explore semantic similarities and differences.

For Clinton, the concept emerged in Topic 6-gun control-and, for Trump, in Topic 1-Supreme Court appointment. Both candidates referred often to the Second Amendment, particularly as it relates to the right to gun ownership. Clinton discussed the need for stronger regulation of guns, particularly with regard to closing certain regulatory loopholes in mandatory background checks, and the words highlighted in the topic clearly reflect this: gun, second, amendment, +loophole, close. However, Trump often suggested his opponent sought to weaken the Second Amendment and, therefore, appointing a new Supreme Court justice who would protect the constitutional right to bear arms would be important. This slant in Trump's speech also was captured by his Topic 1: amendment, +justice, second, +appoint, important.

Although both candidates' negative speech about each other emerged as salient topics in the combined model, slightly different patterns appear in the individual models. Clinton's argument that Trump should release his tax returns emerged in the combined model as Topic 9-tax return (release, +tax, +return, +tax return, audit). In Clinton's model, however, the word "audit" does not appear. It is also interesting to note that tax returns did not emerge as a salient topic in Trump's model, indicating that he largely avoided speaking about the topic; in Clinton's model, the matter of tax returns emerges as Topic 4 (Table 2).

A similar trend emerged with regard to Trump's detraction of Clinton, primarily centered on the use of a private email server during her tenure as Secretary of State. This theme emerged in the general model as Topic 20-email (wrong, +email, +delete, +fact, +know). In Trump's model, this theme is represented in Topic 10 (email, subpoena, +state, congress, united). In the latter, the language captured in the topic is stronger than that captured by the combined model, as Trump overtly called for a subpoena of Clinton's emails and referenced congressional investigations taking place at the time. Conversely, in Clinton's individual model, the term "email" never emerges as salient, suggesting a similar tactic to Trump's, of avoiding discussing potentially unethical matters.

This analysis using separate models for the partitioned data, therefore, demonstrates subtle differences that may not be depicted in a larger combined model. Such refinement of the information lends support for the exploration of topics at different levels of analysis (e.g., splitting a corpus into subgroups, time periods, or contrasting characteristics). In the example above, if so desired, we could further compare the data, to learn how the candidates' responses changed from debate to debate. It could also be informative to compare the models for candidates' stump speeches to these models we generated for the debates, in order to contrast discourses elicited within a fixed, prescribed format with those articulated within more flexible contexts. date, the use of TM has been limited, and mostly exploratory in nature. However, given its ability to unearth the underlying thematic structure of large amounts of data, we contend TM is a powerful tool, applicable to a number of research contexts, especially the social sciences. As a method, TM can quantify data that are otherwise difficult to objectively measure (e.g., language). For example, in the context of LSA, by measuring the angular distance between eigenvectors, one could quantify the relationship between topics, allowing for a deeper semantic understanding. Thus, TM's potential utility falls well beyond its present exploratory scope, across the social sciences.

While not exhaustive, here we propose three social sciences domains in which researchers could employ and expand the use of TM: (a) as a tool for reducing unintentional reviewer bias in systematic literature reviewing, (b) for practical thematic exploration of qualitative data and thematic analysis validation, and (c) for comparing similar corpora to explore semantic similarities and differences.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
sections.header_raw: A Novel Approach to Systematic Literature Reviewing
sections.header_norm: a novel approach to systematic literature reviewing
paragraphs: Literature reviews serve an important role in distilling historical advances in a given field, synthesizing major findings from a large number of studies, and assessing the quality of reported research. More specifically, systematic literature reviews are designed with a priori questions and inclusion criteria, which become a priori constraints or parameters for the subsequent analyses. This emphasis on structure helps reduce researcher-related biases in the collection, inclusion, and analysis processes. The Cochrane Collaboration, a wellknown network of systematic reviewers for medical and health-care literatures, for instance, stresses the importance of clearly formulated questions and strict procedural guidelines as hallmarks of systematic reviews (Cochrane, n.d.).

Although systematic reviewers follow strict guidelines to minimize potential biases, several authors have suggested adding meta-analytic components to all systematic reviews, as a means of reducing bias even further (Brestoff and den Broeck, 2013;Hughes, 1996). Given that TM stands at the intersection of quantitative and qualitative analysis, it offers several important advantages related to minimizing unintentional biases in the review process. Although authors have called for the simultaneous inclusion of meta-analytic statistics in review articles, these statistics are inherently limited to the quantitative findings reported in the reviewed studies. This limitation reflects a somewhat problematic assumption that the most important topics in reviewed studies are their reported statistical findings. However, TM mechanically analyzes the language used in the literature. Thus, it offers a more objective analysis than simple researcher synthesis and is not limited to only statistical output. Further, reporting TM results in a systematic review may serve to reduce perceptions of bias, further validating such studies. Given the recent focus on validation and reduction of bias perceptions in review articles, this method offers an important alternative.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
sections.header_raw: Thematic Exploration of Qualitative Data and Analysis Validation
sections.header_norm: thematic exploration of qualitative data and analysis validation
paragraphs: TM can be an especially useful tool for qualitative researchers. TM can explore underlying themes in a corpus of qualitative data (i.e., can function as a coding mechanism), and can validate researchers' coding structures during analysis. Though modern coding software affords the ability to map and code themes from transcribed the potential for researcher bias remains as decisions about code labels, code definitions, and which segments to chunk and code are the purview of the investigator (Mays and Pope, 1995;Shenton, 2004). And though the issue of unintentional bias is readily addressed with trustworthy remedies recommended by qualitative methods' ethical guidelines (Sanjari et al., 2014), the fact that TM can detect such biases may be appealing to novice and seasoned researchers.

As a coding mechanism, researchers can rely on TM's ability to uncover latent themes and the thematic structure of a corpus of qualitative data. The benefits, here, mimic those we outlined for systematic literature reviewing: rather than rely on human decision making, the computer calculates the most salient components in the corpus. Thus, risk of human error and researchers bias decrease. Moreover, TM can be used as a tool to check the validity of the codes generated by the investigator or by a data analysis team. In this case, an investigator would first code interview transcriptions using standard methods. After coding was completed, the researcher would run a TM on the same data to explore similarities between the model's output and his/her original codes. This process would serve as an interrater reliability check of the coding, thus adding a new layer of trustworthiness or validity (and confidence) to the study's findings.

Using TM in the manners we described above does not eliminate researcher bias, as the naming and interpreting of a topic model's output is subject to many of the same biases found in traditional qualitative research. Further, researcher discretion may still be important in determining the relevance of topics. For instance, in certain cases, important topics may be discussed using fewer words. These topics would appear as less relevant in the topic model, despite their relative importance. Nonetheless, the agreement of a mathematically modeled topic with a researcher's original codes offers greater confidence in the trustworthiness and transferability of findings. Thus, TM holds the potential to contribute significantly to the field if applied in tandem with traditional analytic techniques.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: Comparing corpora involves evaluating two or more collections of documents separately to find differences that otherwise would be unobservable if a combined, single model were run. Similar to the second set of analyses in our example with the presidential election debates, the underlying logic supporting this use of TM stems from the idea that data hold latent themes at different levels of granularity, most of which are not readily apparent. As the information in separate (or divided) corpora are different, we can infer that the topics will also be different, thereby adding a new level to the analysis. Similar to differing levels of information found in second-versus first-order factors in factor analysis, TM offers the same opportunity for exploratory studies.

There may be numerous reasons to analyze different subsets of corpora, but here is a simple example: developing a topic model on all published research literature examining the link between sugar-sweetened beverages and obesity. In general, we would expect to see topics linking sugar to increased risk of obesity. However, if we further hypothesized that funding source might impact research findings, we would then divide the corpus into two subsets, one containing studies funded by governmental agencies and one with studies funded by the sugar industry. We may find, when comparing the rival models, that distinct topics emerge, such as (a) sugar leads to obesity, and (b) sugar does not have a link to obesity, reflecting the distinct biases the funding sources may have created. In such a case, TM serves as an important tool to evaluate the competing (and, often, contradictory) discussions occurring in a given field.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: As with any methodology, is not without limitations. First, because social science TM is in its infancy, debates regarding model fit or predictive use of topics is still evolving. Many of the fit indices or acceptable statistical cutoffs that are common in inferential statistics have not been developed, yet, for TM. Further, the ability to run a topic model is contingent upon having the appropriate amount of computing power to execute the complex computations required. However, these limitations should not deter the interested researcher from learning more about TM (LSA or other forms). Future research should center on exploring TM across multiple corpora of data in the social sciences, and seeking out potential means to determine the accuracy and statistical goodness of fit of the topic model to data.

Second, while more popular TM algorithms exist, such as latent dirichlet allocation (LDA), we purposefully selected LSA for several reasons. First, the language used to explain LSA is similar to explanations of social-scientist-friendly analyses within the general linear model, as opposed to LDA, whose explanation relies heavily on probability and Bayesian statistical theory. Given the exploratory and didactic nature of this article, we believed using language familiar to social scientists (correlation, eigenvectors, among others) would make broader TM both more appealing and accessible to researchers unfamiliar with the methodology. Thus, we highly encourage future researchers to use this collection of data and replicate the analysis with other TM approaches (e.g., LDA) to compare results generated from various methods. Though we would anticipate similar findings, the comparison between models would add to important discussions about the future of text analytics.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2018 - Topic Modeling Latent Semantic Analysis for the Social Sciences
paragraphs: TM represents a potentially useful and valuable innovation for social science research. Herein, we provided a brief overview of the logic underlying TM, a simple example of TM application, and, lastly, a call to expand the use of the method. As a method, TM has been increasing in popularity over the last decade. Until now, however, the majority of its applications are found almost unilaterally in business marketing and social media analyses. We contend that the analytic powers of TM are too valuable not to be applied to the social sciences. Using TM can reduce inherent analytic subjectivity and create new ways to observe (and reduce large amounts of ) text data. In particular, we argue that TM offers unique opportunities for researchers seeking to improve systematic literature reviews, mathematically explore latent themes within large amounts of qualitative data, and compare various corpora of potentially competing data.
sections.text: TM represents a potentially useful and valuable innovation for social science research. Herein, we provided a brief overview of the logic underlying TM, a simple example of TM application, and, lastly, a call to expand the use of the method. As a method, TM has been increasing in popularity over the last decade. Until now, however, the majority of its applications are found almost unilaterally in business marketing and social media analyses. We contend that the analytic powers of TM are too valuable not to be applied to the social sciences. Using TM can reduce inherent analytic subjectivity and create new ways to observe (and reduce large amounts of ) text data. In particular, we argue that TM offers unique opportunities for researchers seeking to improve systematic literature reviews, mathematically explore latent themes within large amounts of qualitative data, and compare various corpora of potentially competing data.
